lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of surgical appliances and supplies, including orthopedic and prosthetic devices, implants, dressings, sutures, medical masks and gloves, specialized hospital furniture, mobility aids, and personal industrial safety devices, with manufacturer-attributable repeat product and service revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Dental equipment and laboratories, general-purpose furniture, electronic hearing aids, protective eyewear, pure distributors and rental businesses, captive internal units, and service businesses that do not manufacture in-scope products.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat outsourced revenue from hospitals, clinics, distributors, rehabilitation providers, industrial users, government buyers, and medical-device OEMs through replenishable supplies, durable appliances, replacement parts, customization, maintenance, and related support.

a.rationale: AI can assist design and customization, documentation, regulatory work, forecasting, procurement, scheduling, inspection support, customer service, and commercial tasks. Sewing, forming, molding, assembly, sterile or validated production, physical fitting, testing, and accountable quality decisions remain less substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across appliance, implant, disposable-supply, furniture, and protective-equipment manufacturers would materially move the range.
caveats: BLS data span all medical equipment and supplies manufacturing and may not reflect this industry's unusually broad material and product mix.
General manufacturing AI use cases do not prove labor savings in regulated or customized product workflows.
a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS reports 338,020 jobs in NAICS 339100, with production occupations at 50.62%, architecture and engineering at 8.79%, business and financial operations at 6.35%, and computer and mathematical work at 2.16%; NIST lists predictive maintenance, quality control, forecasting, scheduling, and design applications. Transformation: assigned substantial exposure to digital and analytical tasks but little to hands-on fabrication, fitting, sterile work, and accountable release. Error direction: broad industry averages may overstate exposure for simple disposables and understate it for customized prosthetics, implants, and engineered equipment.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should advance in office workflows, demand planning, documentation, quality analytics, inspection support, and some design customization. It remains constrained by data quality, legacy systems, skills and capital, cybersecurity, product validation, diverse production processes, and continuing finished-device accountability.
rho.change_evidence: Manufacturer-level adoption data reporting validated deployments, implementation cost, labor hours avoided, error rates, and adoption by product type and firm size would materially refine realization.
caveats: NIST's manufacturer survey and use cases cover industries with different process controls and digital maturity.
Some personal safety products may face less FDA constraint, while implants and many clinical supplies face more.
rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, while identifying data, cost, skills, privacy, cybersecurity, and legacy integration barriers; FDA's QMSR imposes quality-system and risk-management requirements on finished medical-device manufacturers. Transformation: translated broad adoption into the implemented share of exposed tasks over five years and discounted for LMM resources, validation, heterogeneous processes, and regulated accountability. Error direction: stated adoption can overstate operational savings, but rapidly improving packaged tools may raise diffusion above the central case.

e.rationale: Many appliance and supply manufacturers sell repeatedly to external institutions, distributors, industrial users, or OEMs. Eligibility is reduced by captive operations, commodity importers, rental or distribution businesses, custom practitioner shops without repeat outsourced revenue, firms outside the EBITDA band, and businesses dominated by nonmedical safety products.
e.change_evidence: A firm census matched to exact manufactured products, ownership, normalized EBITDA, customer mix, repeat revenue, and manufacturer-versus-distributor status would directly estimate eligibility.
caveats: No public source measures the exact intersection of six-digit activity, independence, recurring external revenue, and the target EBITDA band.
The NAICS scope ranges from commodity dressings and gloves to implants, prosthetics, hospital beds, and protective equipment.

s5.rationale: Founder-owned specialty manufacturers create a plausible transfer pool through succession, sponsor interest, and strategic consolidation. Completed qualifying control transfers exclude minority financings, internal reorganizations, closures, practitioner succession without a transferable manufacturing business, and transactions outside the lens.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year transaction and ownership data matched to product activity, EBITDA, control transfer, seller type, and continuing independent operations would materially move the probability.
caveats: This is a structured estimate without an exact-industry transaction denominator.
Very different buyer universes and consolidation dynamics apply to implants, disposables, prosthetics, furniture, and industrial safety products.

q.rationale: Regulatory files, validated processes, product performance, clinician or user familiarity, customization capability, distributor relationships, and switching costs can protect some benefit. Retention is weaker in commodity dressings, gloves, masks, and safety products, where purchasing groups, distributors, large OEMs, government tenders, imports, and private label create strong price pressure.
q.change_evidence: Product-level evidence on customer concentration, tender structure, distributor rebates, qualification duration, price resets, gross margins, and realized savings retention would materially refine capture.
caveats: Retention varies sharply across differentiated implants, custom appliances, durable equipment, and commodity consumables.
The estimate concerns retained gross benefit rather than reported accounting margin.

d5.rationale: Aging, surgeries, chronic conditions, rehabilitation, replenishment, and workplace-safety needs support demand, while reimbursement, hospital budgets, procedure cycles, inventory normalization, imports, and commodity price competition create downside. The central case follows the broad official medical-equipment output trajectory with a modest discount for volatile supplies.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price shipment or unit forecasts through 2031 for implants, prosthetics, dressings, protective products, mobility aids, and specialized hospital furniture would materially move the ratio.
caveats: BLS projects NAICS 339100 rather than 339113 and does not separate clinical products from industrial safety devices.
Output value can reflect mix and quality changes rather than physical demand available to LMM domestic producers.
d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects medical equipment and supplies manufacturing output from 101.1 to 129.9 over ten years, a 2.5% annual rate. Transformation: used approximately five years of the broad path as an anchor, then widened and slightly reduced the center for commodity supplies, imports, inventory cycles, and the six-digit product mix. Error direction: product mix and inflation-like quality improvements can overstate unit growth, while aging, surgery, chronic care, replenishment, and safety requirements could make demand more durable.

o.rationale: Physical appliances, implants, supplies, mobility aids, hospital furniture, and protective equipment still require an accountable producer. AI may change design, customization, planning, and quality workflows but does not eliminate physical manufacturing; displacement is limited to provider insourcing, direct imports, consolidation, reusable substitution, or therapies and designs that eliminate a product category.
o.change_evidence: Product-level studies of insourcing, import substitution, reusable-versus-disposable shifts, digital fabrication at point of care, and clinical substitution would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
caveats: This estimates persistence of an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not the survival of each incumbent or product line.
On-site additive manufacturing and custom fabrication could shift selected prosthetic or appliance work closer to providers.

narrative.executive_view: Surgical appliances and supplies offer recurring clinical, rehabilitation, and safety demand but span an unusually wide range from commodity consumables to regulated implants and custom prosthetics. AI can improve office, design, planning, and quality workflows; hands-on production and finished-device accountability remain durable.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are documentation, design assistance, customization, forecasting, scheduling, procurement, quality analytics, inspection support, and customer service. Fabrication, sterile processes, fitting, physical testing, and release decisions limit substitution.
narrative.value_capture: Differentiation, validation, clinical familiarity, customization, and channel relationships protect value in some products. Commodity supplies, tenders, distributors, imports, and institutional purchasing sharply reduce capture in others.
narrative.firm_availability: A sizable specialist-manufacturer population is plausible, but product, ownership, recurring-revenue, distributor, practitioner, and size-band screens remove a material share. Exact transfer evidence is absent.
narrative.demand_durability: Broad official output projections support moderate growth, with aging, procedures, chronic care, rehabilitation, replenishment, and safety requirements as drivers. Budgets, reimbursement, imports, inventory cycles, and substitution remain important risks.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal risks are category heterogeneity, recalls and product liability, regulatory compliance, reimbursement, institutional purchasing leverage, commodity pricing, imports, supply-chain quality, inventory cycles, and weak exact-industry transaction evidence.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring care, rehabilitation, replenishment, and workplace-protection needs sustain a broad physical product base.
narrative.principal_weakness: The same code combines highly defensible implants and custom products with price-driven commodity supplies and industrial safety goods.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 339113 Surgical Appliance and Supplies Manufacturing
sources.supports: Exact six-digit scope and examples including orthopedic and prosthetic devices, dressings, sutures, protective devices, hospital beds, and operating-room tables.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Medical Equipment and Supplies Manufacturing
sources.supports: Broad medical-equipment occupational structure, including production, engineering, business, and computer work, used for the task-exposure bridge.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in U.S. Manufacturing: Text Only
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI adoption, use cases, expected expansion, and barriers involving data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy systems.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry
sources.supports: Official ten-year employment and output projection for broad medical equipment and supplies manufacturing.

sources.title: Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)
sources.supports: Finished-device quality-system applicability, ISO 13485 incorporation, risk-management requirements, and inspection framework.
